dominantFinding: 44% forest cover and 1.7 million ha CFR already titled — the largest in India — gives Forest the strongest legal commons infrastructure of all departments; where schemes stall it is not on commons identity but on enforcement, because external mandates (FRA, Social Audit Act) consistently outperform scheme-embedded provisions.
dominantFailure: T4 enforcement requires independent oversight; self-monitoring by scheme implementing agency is consistently weak. Private plantation subsidies (Chief Minister Plantation, Afforestation on Government Land) fail T1 structurally. External legal mandates (Forest Rights Act, Forest Management Plans) achieve T4 compliance better than scheme-embedded provisions.

commonsProfile.keyEcologicalContext: Chhattisgarh holds India's largest CFR titled area (~1.7 million ha); forest commons are legally formalized but T4 enforcement — best delivered by external mandates like FRA and Social Audit Act — remains the binding constraint

structuralFindings.dominantProblem: T4 Sustainability Enforcement Requires External Accountability — internal self-monitoring is insufficient
structuralFindings.socException: Van Dhan Vikas Kendra and Community-Based Forest Management (FRA+JFM integrated) achieve full SOC through formalized commons governance and external sustainability mandates (Social Audit Act, FRA 2006)
crossCuttingPriorities: Commons Legal Framework as SOC Driver: Extend FRA CFR titling to all eligible forest communities (currently <30% coverage); CFR is primary T1 enabler
Independent T4 Monitoring: Social Audits (independent of Forest Department) for all schemes; quarterly monitoring by community-elected observers not departmental staff
Private Land Schemes Structural Redesign: Chief Minister Plantation and Vrikshopan Yojana require fundamental redesign toward community commons land (≥2 ha per 100 HHs)
External Legal Frameworks for T4: Route all forest schemes through FRA-mandated Forest Management Plans rather than scheme-embedded provisions — external law enforces what internal clauses cannot
contextualFactors: Chhattisgarh holds ~44% forest cover — highest structural commons potential among T1 departments; forest commons governance (CFR, JFM) structurally enables SOC achievement
~1.7 million ha CFR already titlized — largest in India; FRA implementation provides ready legal framework for T1 PASS
All private plantation subsidies fail T1 fundamentally; this is a design constraint not fixable without moving expenditure to commons land
Social Audits (independent of Forest Department) create stronger T4 compliance than departmentally-embedded monitoring — institutional independence is the key variable

schemes.name: Forest Rights Act (FRA) - Community Forest Rights (CFR) Implementation
schemes.objective: Formalize and recognize collective forest rights of tribal and forest-dependent communities over scheduled forest areas, enabling community-based forest governance and sustainable livelihood extraction.

evidence.t1: T1.1=B (community ownership via CFR title); T1.2=1 (statutory rights under FRA 2006); T1.3~0.70 (≥70% expenditure on commons assets); T1.4=2 (non-excludable resource benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.90 (CFR beneficiary locality); T2.2~0.85 (ST population ≥80%); T2.3=1 (CFR Committee as mandated governance; Social Audit embedded)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (NTFP extraction/timber/grazing rights formalized); Delta-Y: Rs.8,000+/HH/year (NTFP+timber+grazing); T3.3=3 (forest products/timber/medicinal plants); T3.4=1 (Forest Department and CGMFPFED procurement)
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (Forest Management Plan mandates sustainable yield+reforestation); T4.2~1.0 (E/R ≤1.0 for timber; ≤1.2 for NTFP); T4.3=2 (annual assessments + Social Audit monitoring); T4.4=1 (fund withholding + CFR suspension authority); T4.5=1 (O&M embedded in CFR operations)

upgradePathway: Real-Time Commons Monitoring: Satellite-based forest cover tracking + mobile app for community-reported incidents; dashboard visible to gram sabha
Diversified Offtake: Direct B2C sales and ecotourism revenue routes alongside Forest Department procurement; GI registration for community NTFP products
Climate Finance Integration: Carbon credit revenue stream for CFR areas maintaining/increasing forest cover; community receives 80% of carbon revenue

schemes.name: Joint Forest Management (JFM)
schemes.objective: Establish participatory forest conservation by forming committees of village residents, forest officials, and NGOs to jointly manage degraded forest areas with benefit-sharing.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (state forest with commons governance via JFM Committee); T1.2=1 (community governance structure); T1.3~0.60 (≥60% on common forest restoration); T1.4=2 (non-excludable benefit to all village residents)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.75 (50+ villages per JFM area); T2.2~0.45 (SC/ST beneficiaries ≥40%); T2.3=1 (JFM Committee as formal governance with benefit distribution mechanism)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (NTFP/fuelwood/fodder income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.6,000+/HH/year; T3.3=3 (NTFP/fuelwood/green fodder); T3.4=1 (institutional offtake via Forest Department/CGMFPFED)
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (5-year management plan mandates annual regeneration targets); T4.2~1.2 (E/R ≤1.2 for fuelwood/fodder); T4.3=1 (annual growth assessment + JFM committee reports); T4.4=1 (benefit withholding penalty for non-compliance)

upgradePathway: Benefit Distribution Transparency: Blockchain-based tracking of NTFP collection and revenue distribution per JFM committee member
Climate Finance: Link JFM regeneration areas to verified carbon credits; community retains 80% of credit revenue
Women's Benefit Share: Formalize minimum 50% women's share in JFM benefit distribution; women-led monitoring sub-committee

schemes.name: CAMPA (Compensatory Afforestation Management & Planning Authority)
schemes.objective: Manage funds collected from forest diversion for development projects, channeling capital into reforestation and environmental restoration projects.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (state forest commons); T1.2=1 (community participation in site selection); T1.3~0.85 (entire allocation directed to forest commons restoration); T1.4=2 (non-excludable at community level)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.70 (beneficiaries in diversion-adjacent villages); T2.2~0.40 (SC/ST beneficiaries); T2.3=1 (community participation mandated)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (livelihood: fuelwood/NTFP/fodder from restored forests post-maturation); Delta-Y: Rs.5,000+/HH/year (after 7-10 year maturation); T3.3=3 (multiple pathways); T3.4=1 (institutional buyer: Forest Department)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no mandatory O&M fund — only capital provision); T4.2: no post-project community-based monitoring; T4.3=0 (no extraction rate monitoring); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Mandatory O&M Fund: Earmark 10% of CAMPA capital as 10-year maintenance fund; held in community Forest Committee account
Community Monitoring Committee: Quarterly forest health assessments by elected community members (independent of Forest Department)
Replenishment Rule: Link any harvest to regeneration targets (≥100% replacement planting); trigger automatic benefit reduction if degradation >10%
Penalty Mechanism: Withhold community benefit disbursement if quarterly monitoring shows regeneration shortfall — implement Amrit Sarovar user group model adapted for forests

schemes.name: Minor Forest Produce (NTFP) — Institutional Support & Trading
schemes.objective: Provide institutional framework for forest dwellers to collect, process, and market non-timber forest products (tendu leaf, sal seeds, bamboo, mushrooms, honey) with fair-value procurement guarantees.

evidence.t1: T1.1=B (communal forest with NTFP commons rights); T1.2=1 (community use rights via FRA); T1.3~0.70 (≥60% on commons product collection/processing); T1.4=2 (non-excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.85 (forest-adjacent population); T2.2~0.50 (ST+women-headed HHs ≥50%); T2.3=1 (User Group governance structure)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (NTFP income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.6,500+/HH/year (tendu ₹3,000/sal ₹2,000/bamboo ₹1,500+); T3.3=3 (multiple NTFP pathways); T3.4=1 (institutional buyer: CGMFPFED guarantee)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no sustainable harvest limits codified); T4.2: no monitoring of extraction rates; T4.3=0 (no monitoring framework); T4.4=0; T4.5=0 (no O&M fund for processing infrastructure)

upgradePathway: Extraction Limits: Survey forest NTFP productivity per species; set annual sustainable harvest quotas per gram; link CGMFPFED procurement to quota compliance
Mobile Monitoring System: App-based collection tracking; GPS-tagged collection points; automated flagging of over-collection zones
Replenishment Obligation: Link procurement access to annual regeneration activities (seeding, transplanting) ≥20% of collection area
O&M Fund: Earmark 8% of CGMFPFED revenue to processing infrastructure maintenance and forest regeneration fund

schemes.name: Chief Minister Plantation Scheme
schemes.objective: Provide monetary incentives to private landholders for plantation on personal/revenue land, promoting green cover and individual farmer income.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (private land); T1.2=0 (individual subsidy model); T1.3~0.05 (≥90% on private individual trees); T1.4=0 (highly excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.50 (dispersed beneficiaries); T2.2: absent; T2.3=0 (no commons governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (income not mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.1,000-2,000/year (uncertain tree income); T3.3=0; T3.4=0 (no institutional buyer guarantee)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0; T4.2: no extraction limits; T4.3=0 (no tree survival monitoring); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Community Forestry Commons Model: Gram-level plantation committees on ≥2 ha designated commons land per 100 HHs
Commons Designation: Revenue land transferred to gram sabha commons; plantation is community property with benefit-sharing through rotation
Benefit-Sharing: Annual timber/NTFP income distributed through gram cooperative; SC/ST and women priority
Mandatory Annual Replanting: 100% replacement of any harvested trees; gram committee monitors compliance; subsidy renewal conditional on survival rate ≥80%

schemes.name: Vrikshopan Yojana (Sapling Distribution & Plantation Support)
schemes.objective: Distribute saplings and provide technical support to farmers and communities for tree-planting on individual/community land.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (primarily private land); T1.2=0 (individual farmer distribution); T1.3~0.20 (≥70% saplings to individual farmers); T1.4=0
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.80 (village-level distribution); T2.2~0.45 (SC/ST farmer priority); T2.3=1 (community distribution committees)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no livelihood output mandated); Delta-Y: negligible (long-term uncertain); T3.3=0; T3.4=0
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no tree survival monitoring); T4.2: N/A; T4.3=0 (no monitoring post-distribution); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Community Sapling Bank: Gram-level nurseries managed by plantation committee; saplings grown from local seeds for ecological fit
Commons First: Designate ≥40% of distributed saplings for commons/revenue land plantation (not individual farms)
Conditional Individual Access: Individual farmer allocation conditional on participation in gram commons plantation activities
Mandatory Survival Monitoring: Annual tree survival census; sapling replacements mandatory for <80% survival; removes failed recipients from future eligibility

schemes.name: Bamboo Development Program
schemes.objective: Promote bamboo cultivation through subsidy and market linkages, supporting individual farmers and small industries.

evidence.t1: T1.1=B (bamboo stands on gram commons/private farms; majority on community land); T1.2=1 (community management potential); T1.3~0.60 (≥50% cultivation on community land); T1.4=1 (partial non-excludability)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.70 (village-level beneficiaries); T2.2: no SC/ST priority explicitly; T2.3=0 (private farmer associations, not commons committees)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (income from bamboo mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.4,000-5,000/HH/year; T3.3=1 (bamboo artisan cooperatives); T3.4=1 (government procurement)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no sustainable yield limits); T4.2: no extraction monitoring; T4.3=0 (no O&M fund); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Reorient to Commons Bamboo Groves: ≥60% of scheme budget to community bamboo groves on gram commons land; individual farmers participate through cooperative
Community Committee Governance: Gram bamboo management committee with SC/ST majority; benefit-sharing through rotation harvest
Sustainability Framework: Codify ρ ≤1.2 for bamboo harvest; mandatory replanting ≥100% of harvested culms annually
Revenue Benefit-Sharing: 60% to harvesting members, 30% to O&M fund, 10% to gram sabha commons fund

schemes.name: Forest Management Plan (Operational Framework)
schemes.objective: Develop and implement 10-year management plans for state forests and protected areas, specifying conservation priorities, resource extraction limits, and community livelihood integration.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (state forest commons); T1.2=1 (community governance framework mandated); T1.3~0.80 (≥70% on commons conservation); T1.4=2 (non-excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.75 (forest-dependent villages prioritized); T2.2~0.45 (ST participation); T2.3=1 (forest committees with community representation)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no mandated livelihood output indicator); Delta-Y: unknown (NTFP extraction permissive, not guaranteed); T3.3=0 (benefit extraction secondary to conservation); T3.4=0
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (plan mandates regeneration targets annually); T4.2=1 (sustainable yield limits specified per species); T4.3=2 (5-yearly forest inventory + community monitoring); T4.4=1 (plan revision + budget reallocation penalties)

upgradePathway: Specify NTFP Income Targets: Each FMP must include community livelihood annex with Delta-Y ≥₹5,000/HH/year target and annual progress tracking
Formalize Extraction Rights: Codify NTFP and fuelwood quantities per village as legal rights within FMP; not just permissive but guaranteed
Institutional Offtake: Link FMP NTFP rights to CGMFPFED guaranteed procurement; FMP becomes legal basis for procurement agreement
Livelihood Monitoring: Quarterly beneficiary income tracking; annual community income audit; FMP renewal conditional on livelihood targets met

schemes.name: Van Dhan Vikas Kendra (NTFP Value Addition)
schemes.objective: Establish village-level value-addition units for forest products, creating employment and processing infrastructure for tribal women and forest dwellers.
schemes.classification_note: Cross-departmental scheme also tracked under Tribal Affairs Department

evidence.t1: T1.1=B (forest NTFP commons under FRA rights); T1.2=1 (NTFP rights via FRA 2006); T1.3~0.85 (≥80% on commons processing infrastructure); T1.4=2 (all members share benefit equally)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.95 (strictly village-level tribal communities); T2.2~0.80 (tribal women ≥80%); T2.3=1 (Women SHG governance structure)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (income from NTFP value addition mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.10,000-12,000/HH/year (processing markup 3-5x raw price); T3.3=3 (honey/herbal/paste/medicines); T3.4=1 (CGMFPFED + B2C sales)
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (procurement linked to forest regeneration quotas); T4.2~1.0 (sustainable harvesting enforced); T4.3=2 (quarterly NTFP sourcing audits); T4.4=1 (unit closure if extraction exceeds limits); T4.5=1 (operational budget includes monitoring+restoration)

upgradePathway: Scale Horizontally: Expand from 50+ to 200+ units by 2028; focus on underserved PVTG areas
Direct Market Contracts: Formalize supply agreements with Ayush Ministry, export companies, and online platforms; bypass intermediaries
Real-Time Sourcing Monitoring: IoT + blockchain for collection-to-processing tracking; transparency for premium buyers
Climate Finance: Carbon credits for Van Dhan catchment forests; community retains 80% of credit revenue

schemes.name: Ecotourism Development
schemes.objective: Develop managed tourism in forest protected areas, generating revenue for conservation and local community livelihoods through guided tours, homestays, and handicraft sales.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (protected area landscape commons); T1.2=1 (community infrastructure on commons); T1.3~0.65 (≥60% on commons tourism infrastructure); T1.4=1
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.70 (village-level benefit); T2.2: no SC/ST prioritization; T2.3=0 (Tourism Department + private operators, not community committees)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (guide employment/homestay/handicraft income); Delta-Y: Rs.10,000+/year; T3.3=3 (guide/homestay/handicraft); T3.4=1 (tourist demand offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no visitor carrying capacity limits); T4.2: no ecological impact monitoring; T4.3=0 (no O&M fund); T4.4=0 (no revenue-to-regeneration mandate); T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Community Tourism Committee Governance: Village community holds tourism rights; SC/ST majority in management; replaces Tourism Department + private operator model
Carrying Capacity Limits: Annual visitor cap based on ecological assessment; enforcement condition for tourism license renewal
Revenue-Sharing for Regeneration: 20% of all tourism revenue to forest restoration fund managed by gram committee
Ecological Monitoring: Quarterly biodiversity assessments by independent agency; tourism suspended if degradation threshold triggered

schemes.name: Vana Samridhi (Forest Enrichment Program)
schemes.objective: Enhance forest productivity through selective species plantation, tending, and regeneration of degraded forest patches, improving timber yield and NTFP productivity.

evidence.t1: T1.1=A (state forest commons); T1.2=1 (commons enrichment on public land); T1.3~0.80 (≥75% on commons enrichment); T1.4=2 (non-excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.80 (forest village beneficiaries); T2.2~0.45 (ST priority); T2.3=1 (gram committee involvement mandated)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no livelihood mandate; enhancement secondary to conservation); Delta-Y: 0 (no guaranteed benefit distribution); T3.3=0; T3.4=0
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (program mandates 100% regeneration of harvested area); T4.2=1 (regeneration targets embedded in 5-year work plans); T4.3=2 (5-yearly growth assessments); T4.4=1 (fund withholding if regeneration <80%)

upgradePathway: Mandate Livelihood Output: Every Vana Samridhi area must project community income Delta-Y ≥₹5,000/HH/year from NTFP/fuelwood post-maturation; annual tracking
Formalize Benefit Rights: Specify NTFP and timber income shares per village in work plan; convert permissive access to guaranteed community right
Benefit Distribution Mechanism: Annual income audit; gram committee distributes NTFP revenue; women's share minimum 40%
Employment Guarantee: Forest village youth priority for enrichment labor; link to MGNREGS for wage guarantee

schemes.name: Community-Based Forest Management (Integrated with FRA + JFM)
schemes.objective: Comprehensive framework combining Forest Rights Act (CFR titles), Joint Forest Management committees, and community-led forest restoration, enabling integrated commons governance.
schemes.classification_note: Integrated governance framework combining FRA and JFM — not a standalone scheme

evidence.t1: T1.1=B (community Category B CFR title); T1.2=1 (FRA-certified ownership); T1.3~0.80 (≥70% on commons governance and restoration); T1.4=2 (non-excludable)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.92 (CFR = community village boundary); T2.2~0.85 (ST-dominant population); T2.3=1 (CFR Committee + Gram Sabha governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (NTFP+timber+grazing+wage labor income mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.8,000+/HH/year; T3.3=3 (multiple forest product pathways); T3.4=1 (institutional buyers: Forest Dept/TRIFED)
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (Forest Management Plans + Social Audit mandate replenishment); T4.2=1 (mandatory replenishment in FMP); T4.3=2 (annual monitoring + Social Audit); T4.4=1 (penalties for violations — rights suspension); T4.5=1 (O&M embedded in CFR governance)

upgradePathway: Scale Integration: Currently ≤30% of forest area under CBFM; target 80% by 2030 through accelerated CFR titling
Unified Framework: Integrate all 13 Forest Department schemes under single CFR-anchored CBFM umbrella by 2028
Technology Overlay: Satellite monitoring dashboard visible to all CFR communities; real-time deforestation alerts
Carbon Finance: Register all CBFM areas for voluntary carbon credits; community premium income supplements NTFP revenue

schemes.name: Afforestation on Government Land (Non-CPR)
schemes.objective: Afforest non-forest state land (revenue land, urban periphery) with individual tree benefits (fruit, timber) directed to local communities or sold for state revenue.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (government non-forest land; excludable benefit to state treasury or individual lessees); T1.2=0; T1.3~0.05; T1.4=0
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.50 (state treasury as beneficiary; no community targeting); T2.2: absent; T2.3=0
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (revenue to state, not livelihood mandate); Delta-Y: 0 (no community income pathway); T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Community Commons Afforestation: Vest government revenue land in nearest gram panchayat as commons land; plantation becomes community property
Community Management: Gram committee manages plantation through 5-year rotation; benefit-sharing with all gram residents
Sustainability Monitoring: Quarterly tree survival census; annual harvest/replanting audit; gram committee accountability
Income Target: Community plantation cooperative must achieve ₹5,000+/HH/year within 10 years of maturation
